title: Recording pig weights through the finish
subtitle: Tracking liveweight growth to hit your target deadweight.
excerpt: Weaner pigs that are weighed every two weeks give you control over the finishing date. A pig that is growing faster than expected can go to the abattoir earlier; one that has stalled can have its ration adjusted. Without a weight record, the booking date is a guess.
sourceNotes: AHDB pig finishing targets and liveweight guide, ahdb.org.uk. BPEX Better Returns Programme. Soil Association outdoor pig production guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: The total weight of a pig on the scale while alive. Deadweight at slaughter is typically 75 to 80 percent of liveweight for a well-finished pig.
An estimate of liveweight from a heart-girth measurement using a calibrated measuring tape. Accurate to within 5 to 10 percent and avoids the need for a weighing crate.

content.text: A weaner arriving at roughly 25 kg should reach a target

of 85 to 100 kg for pork and 100 to 120 kg for bacon over approximately 16 to 24 weeks, depending on the breed and ration. The most important thing the weight record tells you is whether daily liveweight gain is on track.
A purpose-built pig-weighing crate is the most accurate method but costs more than many small-scale setups justify. The alternative is
tutorial for the method. The tape is calibrated for the conversion from heart girth circumference to liveweight. The reading is approximate but sufficient for management decisions.

Weigh every two weeks from arrival to approximately six weeks before target finish date, then weekly. Record date, pig ID (tag number or name), weight, and daily liveweight gain since the last recording.

A healthy, well-fed growing pig gains 600 to 900 g per day. If the gain falls below 500 g per day and stays there over two recording periods, something is wrong. Check ration quality, health, worm burden, whether pen-mates are competing at the feeder, and whether cold or overcrowding is a factor.

Book the abattoir slot six to eight weeks ahead of when you expect the pig to be at target weight. Most abattoirs for small producers are booked weeks in advance. A pig that reaches target weight while the abattoir slot is still three weeks away will be overfat by slaughter; having the data lets you book early enough.

Use the weight data to adjust ration if needed. A pig growing faster than the target curve can be moved to a lower-energy finisher ration four to six weeks before slaughter to reduce back fat without reducing lean growth. A pig growing slowly needs investigation before a ration change, check first whether the problem is health or management.
